parsed.StudyDescription: This dataset combines two related studies: Study 1 (2019) focused on consonant-vowel (CV) and vowel-consonant (VC) phoneme pairs, while Study 2 (2021) expanded to include single phonemes and phoneme triplets (CVC). Both studies employed TMS to targeted areas of the motor cortex during a phoneme discrimination task.

title: MUSIN-G music listening EEG
fit: Natural music self-supervised EEG tokenization; weak affect labels

parsed.Instructions: Instructions before the presentation of the songs: Your task is to listen to all the songs carefully and answer questions regarding the songs. Close your eyes when you hear a sound signal. The song will then be played. Open your eyes only after you hear a sound signal. Answer the questions related to the song. The entire process will be repeated till all the songs get over. Press the spacebar to continue. Instructions to rate familiarity: How familiar are you with this song? press 1 for extremely familiar, press 2 for familiar, press 3 for neither familiar nor unfamiliar, press 4 for unfamiliar, and press 5 for extremely unfamiliar.  Instructions to rate enjoyment How much did you enjoy this song?  press 1 for extremely enjoyable, press 2 for enjoyable, press 3 for neither enjoyable nor unenjoyable, press 4 for unenjoyable, and press 5 for extremely unenjoyable.
parsed.TaskDescription: The data was collected on 20 undergraduate and postgraduate students of IIT Gandhinagar (16 males, 04 females), with an average age of 25.3+/-3.38. Participants had little to no musical training and hence were considered non-musicians

parsed.TaskDescription: Speech decoding task (minimally overt). Participants produced one of 5 color words (green, magenta, orange, violet, yellow) in minimally overt speech condition. Each trial consists of 5 repetitions of the same word (1.25 sec per repetition, 6.25 sec total). Session type: calibration.

title: Auditory Attention Detection Dataset KULeuven
fit: Classic envelope/AAD benchmark; use strict trial/story split

lines: Please cite the original paper where this data set was presented:

Biesmans, W., Das, N., Francart, T., & Bertrand, A. (2016). Auditory-inspired speech envelope extraction methods for improved EEG-based auditory attention detection in a cocktail party scenario. IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering, 25(5), 402-412.

We have observed the widespread utilization of this dataset in numerous research papers, establishing it as a standard benchmark for evaluating novel decoding strategies in auditory attention decoding (AAD).

We wish to underscore the vital importance of conducting rigorous cross-validation in such investigations. In the original study by Biesmans et al., which produced this dataset, linear correlation-based methods were employed, and a straightforward random cross-validation sufficed. However, with the recent surge in the application of machine learning techniques, particularly deep neural networks, in tackling the AAD challenge, a more stringent cross-validation approach becomes imperative. Deep networks are susceptible to overfitting to trial-specific patterns in EEG data, even from very brief segments (less than 1 second), leading to the ability to identify the trial source. Since a subject typically maintains attention to the same speaker throughout a trial, having knowledge of the trial effectively results in a perfect attention decoding.

We observed that many research papers utilizing our dataset still adhere to the basic random cross-validation method, neglecting the separation of trials into training and testing sets. Consequently, these studies frequently report remarkably high AAD accuracies when using extremely short EEG segments (one or a few seconds). Nevertheless, research has demonstrated that such an approach yields inaccurate and excessively optimistic outcomes. Accuracies often plummet significantly, sometimes even falling below chance levels, when employing a proper cross-validation where this trial bias is removed (e.g., leave-one-trial-out, leave-one-story-out, or leave-one-subject-out cross-validation).
This overfitting effect is described in:

title: ESAA: an EEG-Speech auditory attention detection database
fit: Mandarin tonal-language AAD; useful after core OpenNeuro sets

lines: This ESAA database consists of EEG data for 17 normal-hearing subjects. Taking S1/S1.mat as an example, the detailed descriptions of each file can be summarized as follows:
ChannelCount: The total number of EEG channels for data collection
Channels: Assigning the coordinates  for each channel in the spherical coordinate
Markers: Providing the description and position for each Marker along the timeline

The experiment contains 32 trials per subject and each trial lasted 55 to 90 seconds. To avoid fatigue or lack of concentration, the subjects took a short break after each trial and took longer breaks after 8 successive trials. Throughout the experiments, the position of the target streams as well as the gender of the speaker were randomized subject by subject.
trail1S: Start of the first trial
trail1E:  End of the first trial
Others: EEG channel and the data collected from this channel
